accept: act init creates .act/ AND a single git commit by default; commit subject is 'act init: tracker initialized'.
Only .act and .gitignore are staged — pre-existing dirty work in the tree stays untouched.
Auto-commit failure (e.g. dirty conflicting state) is reported but does not roll back the on-disk init; user can commit manually.
Test asserts RunInit(commit=true) leaves a single new commit on the current branch with the canonical subject.
payload.description:
Per Andrew 2026-05-10: 'should the project owner who calls act init be expected to commit .act/? Couldn't that just be part of act init?' — yes. The agent-first thesis says humans interact through agents; requiring a manual commit step after act init is friction that doesn't serve them.

Implementation:
- act init now stages '.act' and '.gitignore' specifically (never -A, so dirty work in the tree stays out) and creates one commit with subject 'act init: tracker initialized'.
- New --no-commit flag opts out for users who want manual control.
- successOutput JSON includes 'committed': true|false and 'commit_error' (only when commit was requested but failed; the on-disk state is still valid).
- Human output now follows up with 'Committed initial state ...' on success, or a 'warning: ...; commit manually with: git add ...' line on commit-failure.
- MCP-driven init defaults to commit=true (callers don't have to chain a manual commit).
payload.title: act init auto-commits .act/ + .gitignore by default
